codeReview.summary:
The implementation is thorough and well-structured. All five requirements are covered: (1) QueryModel TypeScript class with add/remove predicates, auto-generated variable names, variable joining via connectVariable/forceJoin, undo/redo history stack, and Datalog serialisation; (2) VisualQueryBuilder renders a readable sentence-like display with colour-coded shared variables; (3) Clicking a predicate in PredicateBrowser adds it via SidebarPredicateBrowser wrapper; (4) Each predicate block has an accessible remove button; (5) toDatalog() serialises to valid Datalog with a proper head/body. The ReactContext architecture for the QueryModel (QueryContext + useQuery hook in separate files) correctly follows the project convention. Tests are comprehensive: 35 QueryModel unit tests + 19 VisualQueryBuilder component tests covering all major behaviours. TypeScript, ESLint, and all 86 tests pass.

Two non-blocking issues were found: a logic gap in head-variable calculation for queries without joins, and code duplication of collectHeadVars.

issues.description: collectHeadVars falls back to emitting ALL variables in the query head when none are shared (i.e., the query has no joins). For a valid Datalog rule with no joins this is technically correct (all variables are free), but it is inconsistent with what a user likely expects: `ans(x, y, z, s) :- Study(s)` when Study has only one parameter. For a non-join query the user probably wants only the variables they care about in the head, but the feature spec doesn't define this edge case so this is a design ambiguity rather than a bug. No action required unless a future feature adds variable selection.

The private helper computeHeadVars is a verbatim copy of collectHeadVars from QueryModel.ts (only the function name differs). If the head-variable logic ever changes in QueryModel, the component copy will silently diverge, causing the 'Find …' sentence display to be inconsistent with the Datalog serialisation preview in the same component. Consider exporting collectHeadVars from QueryModel.ts and importing it in VisualQueryBuilder.tsx to eliminate the duplication. No functional bug today, but a latent maintenance risk.

The module-level _idCounter is never reset between QueryModel instances (only via the exported resetIdCounter() helper called in tests). In production there is only one QueryProvider so this is fine, but if two QueryProvider instances were mounted simultaneously (e.g., in a future Storybook story or a test file that doesn't call resetIdCounter), predicate IDs would not be deterministic. This is correctly handled in both test files with beforeEach(() => resetIdCounter()), but the reliance on global mutable state is a fragile design pattern.

computeSharedVariables is called unconditionally on every render of QueryProvider (i.e., on every forceUpdate call). For large queries with many predicates this could be a performance concern. A useMemo with state.predicates as dependency would avoid recomputation on unrelated re-renders. This is unlikely to matter for typical neuroimaging query sizes, but is worth noting.

The 'returns empty map when no variables are shared' test (computeSharedVariables describe block) adds predicates 'A' and 'B' with params ['a'] and ['b'] respectively. The auto-generated variable names will both be 'a' (first letter of 'a') and the second will be 'a1' (collision avoidance), so they will NOT collide. But the test comment says 'unless by coincidence the auto-names collide' and then checks only that any color found is from the palette — it doesn't actually assert that the map is empty. This means the test would pass even if a bug caused spurious sharing. A stricter assertion (expect(map.size).toBe(0)) would make the test more meaningful. Non-blocking since the logic is tested elsewhere.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The worker discovered that the schema API returns ordinal integer parameter names (0, 1, 2, 3) instead of semantic names for predicates like PeakReported. This causes auto-generated variable names to fall back to the generic 'v' prefix (genVarName returns 'v' when letters are empty after stripping non-alpha chars from '0', '1', etc.). The handoff's discoveredIssues section notes this and suggests updating v2_handlers.py to use predicate_parameter_names(). This knowledge — that the schema API returns integer param names for certain predicates — is not documented in architecture.md or environment.md and could trip up the next worker implementing variable renaming or query execution.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: QueryModel.ts:62 — genVarName('0', 0) returns 'v' (fallback branch). Handoff discoveredIssues[0]: 'schema API returns parameter names as integers (0, 1, 2, 3) for predicates like PeakReported'. architecture.md and environment.md contain no mention of this schema API behaviour.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The worker correctly followed the AGENTS.md convention of separating the context provider (QueryContext.tsx) and the consumer hook (useQuery.ts) into separate files to satisfy the react-refresh lint rule about mixed exports. This convention is already documented in AGENTS.md so no gap, but it's worth confirming future workers read AGENTS.md before creating context files.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: AGENTS.md: 'React context pattern: Provider components go in src/context/FooContext.tsx, hooks go in src/context/useFoo.ts'. QueryContext.tsx and useQuery.ts match this convention exactly.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The skill (frontend-worker SKILL.md step 6) requires manual UI verification via agent-browser after implementation. The worker performed manual interactive verification but did not explicitly invoke the agent-browser skill — they appear to have verified by starting the dev server and running it locally. The handoff.skillFeedback.followedProcedure is true and deviations is empty, but the skill's step 6 says to 'Use agent-browser to navigate to the relevant page/component'. This may indicate the agent-browser skill was used implicitly in the session or the worker chose direct verification instead. The skill might benefit from clarifying that direct dev-server verification is acceptable for non-visual-regression testing.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Handoff interactiveChecks shows manual verification of clicks and undo/redo. skillFeedback.deviations is empty. Skill SKILL.md step 6 says 'Use agent-browser skill'. No agent-browser tool calls visible in transcript skeleton.

summary: The visual-query-builder-core feature is well-implemented and complete. All six expected behaviours are delivered: predicate click-to-add, readable sentence rendering, shared variable colour highlighting, per-predicate remove button, undo/redo, and Datalog serialisation. The code is clean, the architecture follows project conventions, and 86 tests all pass with TypeScript and lint clean. Five non-blocking issues were noted: a head-variable fallback edge case (design ambiguity, not a bug), code duplication of the collectHeadVars helper between QueryModel and VisualQueryBuilder, reliance on a module-level ID counter with a non-obvious reset API, a missing useMemo for sharedVarColors, and a weak test assertion in the computeSharedVariables empty-map test. One shared-state knowledge gap was identified: the fact that the schema API returns integer ordinal parameter names for some predicates (causing generic 'v' variable names) should be documented in architecture.md so future workers know to handle this case.
